elements.name: Title
texts: "A rock, which, rising to the surface of the water, the breeze had exposed"

elements.name: Description
texts: Knox reveals that the "serpent" is a rock

elements.name: Source
texts: Pastoral Annals. By an Irish Clergyman [i.e. James S. Knox], p. 392

elements.name: Publisher
texts: R. B. Seeley and W. Burnside, London

elements.name: Contributor
texts: Digitised by Google, sponsored by Princeton Library

elements.name: Text
texts: "As it had seemed to me, so [the lake serpent] in fact turned out; a rock, which, rising to the surface of the water, the breeze had exposed. I brought the boat close alongside, and stepped upon it with no other evil consequences, then, or since, to rebuke my temerity, than a partial wetting of my feet. The matter being now placed beyond question, I earnestly besought the boatman to follow me. But this they requested to be excused from; and as I was almost unknown to them, and consequently possessed no personal influence which might have counterbalanced their apprehensions, I desisted from the hopeless endeavour. In truth, the awe of their recent panic was upon the."
